interpretations.interpretation: A straightforward reading of the genealogy: the text is taken as recording that Jeconiah (and his brothers) belonged to Josiah’s family line, with their birth associated with the period of the Babylonian deportation.

A contextual/chronological reading: the audience may understand the phrase “upon the carrying away to Babylon” as indicating that Jeconiah and his brothers were born (or came into prominence) during/after the deportation, even if this compresses dates—so the emphasis is on the exile setting rather than strict royal chronology.

A flexible use of “begat”: some readers might treat “Josiah begat Jeconiah” as meaning “from Josiah’s line came Jeconiah and his brothers” (i.e., ancestry/household connection), rather than insisting on a strictly direct biological father-son relationship.

A textual or transmission-based possibility: given that genealogies in older manuscripts can vary, contemporaries might attribute the wording to copying or variant tradition, while still understanding the passage’s purpose as linking Jeconiah’s family to Josiah’s dynasty.

A theological-historical emphasis: readers could see the line as underscoring that the Davidic family and God’s covenantal story continue even amid Babylon’s conquest and the community’s displacement—exile does not erase the lineage.

A fulfillment-of-prophecy interpretation: because Babylonian deportation is central in Israel’s prophetic memory, the audience might read the notice as fitting the prophetic narrative of judgment and its aftermath, with genealogical detail serving as a historical “proof” that events unfolded as foretold.
